foundations-single-number.title: Single Number
foundations-single-number.watch: don't reach for a hashmap; XOR is the O(1)-space answer they want.

foundations-number-of-1-bits.title: Number of 1 Bits
foundations-number-of-1-bits.insight: `n & (n-1)` removes one set bit per step.
foundations-number-of-1-bits.watch: in C# use `uint` to avoid sign issues, or `BitOperations.PopCount`.

foundations-counting-bits.title: Counting Bits
foundations-counting-bits.watch: this is secretly a DP problem.

foundations-single-number-ii.title: Single Number II (every element thrice but one)
foundations-single-number-ii.approach: sum bits per position across all numbers; bits with count%3≠0 belong to the answer.
foundations-single-number-ii.watch: the elegant version uses two accumulators (ones/twos) — know it exists.

arrays-and-hashing-two-sum.title: Two Sum
arrays-and-hashing-two-sum.insight: for each x, the partner you need is `target - x`; remember numbers you've passed.
arrays-and-hashing-two-sum.approach: one pass; before inserting x, check if its complement is already in the map.
arrays-and-hashing-two-sum.watch: check complement *before* inserting current, or you'll match an element with itself.

arrays-and-hashing-contains-duplicate.title: Contains Duplicate
arrays-and-hashing-contains-duplicate.insight: a set tells you membership in O(1).
arrays-and-hashing-contains-duplicate.approach: add as you go, return true on first re-add.
arrays-and-hashing-contains-duplicate.watch: O(1)-space alternative is sort-then-scan at O(n log n) — mention the trade.

arrays-and-hashing-group-anagrams.title: Group Anagrams
arrays-and-hashing-group-anagrams.approach: key = sorted string *or* a 26-length count vector; group into a `Dictionary<string, List<string>>`.
arrays-and-hashing-group-anagrams.complexity: O(n·k log k) with sorted key, O(n·k) with count key (k = word length).
arrays-and-hashing-group-anagrams.watch: the count-vector key avoids the sort and is the better answer.

arrays-and-hashing-top-k-frequent-elemen.title: Top K Frequent Elements
arrays-and-hashing-top-k-frequent-elemen.insight: you don't need a full sort.
arrays-and-hashing-top-k-frequent-elemen.approach: frequency map → bucket array indexed by count (bucket sort), collect from high counts; O(n). Or a size-k min-heap, O(n log k).
arrays-and-hashing-top-k-frequent-elemen.complexity: O(n) bucket / O(n log k) heap.
arrays-and-hashing-top-k-frequent-elemen.watch: bucket sort beats the heap here and surprises interviewers.

arrays-and-hashing-product-of-array-exce.title: Product of Array Except Self
arrays-and-hashing-product-of-array-exce.insight: answer[i] = (product of everything left) × (product of everything right).
arrays-and-hashing-product-of-array-exce.approach: prefix pass storing left-products, suffix pass multiplying right-products in place.
arrays-and-hashing-product-of-array-exce.watch: division is banned (and breaks on zeros) — that's the whole point.

arrays-and-hashing-valid-sudoku.title: Valid Sudoku
arrays-and-hashing-valid-sudoku.insight: validity = no duplicate within any row, column, or 3×3 box.
arrays-and-hashing-valid-sudoku.approach: 9 sets each for rows/cols/boxes; box index = `(r/3)*3 + c/3`.
arrays-and-hashing-valid-sudoku.watch: the box-index formula is the only tricky part.

arrays-and-hashing-longest-consecutive-s.title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
arrays-and-hashing-longest-consecutive-s.insight: only *start counting* a run from a number whose predecessor is absent.
arrays-and-hashing-longest-consecutive-s.approach: dump into a set; for each x with no `x-1` in set, walk x, x+1, x+2… counting.
arrays-and-hashing-longest-consecutive-s.complexity: O(n) (each number visited at most twice).
arrays-and-hashing-longest-consecutive-s.watch: without the "only start at run-beginnings" guard it degrades to O(n²).

arrays-and-hashing-first-missing-positiv.title: First Missing Positive
arrays-and-hashing-first-missing-positiv.insight: the answer is in [1, n+1]; use the array indices as a hash.
arrays-and-hashing-first-missing-positiv.approach: place each value v into slot v-1 by swapping (cyclic sort); then the first slot where `a[i] != i+1` gives the answer.
arrays-and-hashing-first-missing-positiv.watch: ignore values ≤0 or >n; the in-place swap loop is the trick that achieves O(1) space.

two-pointers-valid-palindrome.title: Valid Palindrome
two-pointers-valid-palindrome.approach: skip non-alphanumerics, lowercase, compare l/r inward.
two-pointers-valid-palindrome.watch: the filtering (`char.IsLetterOrDigit`) is where bugs hide, not the pointer logic.

two-pointers-two-sum-ii-sorted.title: Two Sum II (sorted)

two-pointers-3sum.title: 3Sum
two-pointers-3sum.insight: fix one number, then it's Two-Sum-sorted on the rest.
two-pointers-3sum.approach: sort; for each i, run converging pointers on (i+1, end) for target `-a[i]`; **skip duplicates** at i and after each match.
two-pointers-3sum.watch: duplicate handling is the whole difficulty — skip equal values at all three positions.

two-pointers-container-with-most-water.title: Container With Most Water
two-pointers-container-with-most-water.insight: area is bounded by the *shorter* wall, so moving the taller one can never help.
two-pointers-container-with-most-water.approach: converge; always move the shorter pointer inward, track max area.
two-pointers-container-with-most-water.watch: prove why moving the shorter wall is safe — that's the interview question behind the question.

two-pointers-sort-colors-dutch-flag.title: Sort Colors (Dutch flag)
two-pointers-sort-colors-dutch-flag.insight: three regions (0s, 1s, 2s) maintained by three pointers.
two-pointers-sort-colors-dutch-flag.approach: `low`, `mid`, `high`; mid scans, swap 0s to front, 2s to back; don't advance mid after swapping with high.
two-pointers-sort-colors-dutch-flag.watch: after swapping `mid` with `high`, *don't* increment mid (the swapped-in value is unexamined).

two-pointers-trapping-rain-water.title: Trapping Rain Water
two-pointers-trapping-rain-water.insight: water above index i = min(maxLeft, maxRight) − height[i].
two-pointers-trapping-rain-water.approach: two pointers tracking running maxLeft/maxRight; advance the side with the smaller wall (it bounds the water).
two-pointers-trapping-rain-water.watch: know all three solutions (DP-prefix-max, stack, two-pointer); two-pointer is the O(1)-space flex.

sliding-window-longest-substring-without.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
sliding-window-longest-substring-without.insight: window is valid while all chars are unique.
sliding-window-longest-substring-without.approach: on duplicate, shrink left past the previous occurrence.
sliding-window-longest-substring-without.watch: using a last-seen-index map lets you jump left in O(1) instead of shrinking one step at a time.

sliding-window-longest-repeating-charact.title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
sliding-window-longest-repeating-charact.insight: window is valid while `(windowLength − countOfMostFrequentChar) ≤ k` (those are the chars you'd replace).
sliding-window-longest-repeating-charact.approach: track char counts and the running max frequency; shrink when the replacement budget is exceeded.
sliding-window-longest-repeating-charact.watch: you don't need to recompute maxFreq on shrink — a slightly stale max still yields a correct answer (classic subtlety).

sliding-window-minimum-window-substring.title: Minimum Window Substring
sliding-window-minimum-window-substring.insight: expand until the window covers all required chars, then contract to minimize while it still covers.
sliding-window-minimum-window-substring.approach: need-map of target counts + a `have/need` counter; expand right, and while satisfied, record length and shrink left.
sliding-window-minimum-window-substring.watch: this is the hardest standard window — get the `have==need` bookkeeping exactly right.

sliding-window-best-time-to-buy-sell-sto.title: Best Time to Buy/Sell Stock
sliding-window-best-time-to-buy-sell-sto.insight: it's a degenerate window — track the minimum price seen and the best profit against it.
sliding-window-best-time-to-buy-sell-sto.approach: one pass, `min = Math.Min(min, price)`, `best = Math.Max(best, price - min)`.

sliding-window-permutation-in-string.title: Permutation in String
sliding-window-permutation-in-string.insight: a permutation = a fixed-size window with matching character counts.
sliding-window-permutation-in-string.approach: fixed window of len(target); slide and compare count vectors.
sliding-window-permutation-in-string.watch: compare 26-length vectors, not sorted strings, to stay O(1) per check.

sliding-window-sliding-window-maximum.title: Sliding Window Maximum
sliding-window-sliding-window-maximum.insight: maintain a deque of *indices* whose values are decreasing; the front is always the window max.
sliding-window-sliding-window-maximum.approach: pop smaller values from the back before pushing; pop the front when it slides out of range.
sliding-window-sliding-window-maximum.watch: store indices (to detect expiry), not values; this is the bridge into monotonic structures.

prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-subarra.title: Subarray Sum Equals K
prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-subarra.insight: subarray (l,r] sums to k iff `pre[r] − pre[l] = k`, i.e. a prior prefix equals `pre[r] − k`.
prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-subarra.watch: seed the map with `{0:1}` for subarrays starting at index 0; handles negatives where sliding window can't.

prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-range-s.title: Range Sum Query 2D (Immutable)
prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-range-s.insight: 2D prefix where `pre[i][j]` = sum of the rectangle from origin.
prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-range-s.approach: inclusion-exclusion to build and to query.
prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-range-s.watch: the four-term inclusion-exclusion (add two, subtract two… plus the corner) is the only tricky part.

prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-corpora.title: Corporate Flight Bookings / Car Pooling
prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-corpora.insight: a range `+v` over [l,r] = `diff[l] += v; diff[r+1] -= v`, then prefix-sum the diff array.
prefix-sum-and-difference-arrays-corpora.approach: apply all updates as endpoints, sweep once.

binary-search-binary-search.title: Binary Search
binary-search-binary-search.insight: the canonical loop; get boundaries perfect.
binary-search-binary-search.watch: memorize the half-open template so you never re-derive it under pressure.

binary-search-search-in-rotated-sorted-a.title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
binary-search-search-in-rotated-sorted-a.insight: at any mid, *one half is still sorted* — determine which, then decide if target lies in it.
binary-search-search-in-rotated-sorted-a.approach: compare `a[mid]` to `a[lo]` to identify the sorted half; narrow accordingly.
binary-search-search-in-rotated-sorted-a.watch: duplicates break the "which half is sorted" test (that's the harder II variant).

binary-search-find-first-and-last-positi.title: Find First and Last Position
binary-search-find-first-and-last-positi.insight: it's two boundary searches — lower bound of target and lower bound of target+1 minus one.
binary-search-find-first-and-last-positi.watch: reuse the exact template; don't write two bespoke searches.

binary-search-koko-eating-bananas.title: Koko Eating Bananas
binary-search-koko-eating-bananas.insight: "minimum speed such that she finishes in H hours" is monotonic in speed → binary-search the speed.
binary-search-koko-eating-bananas.approach: feasible(speed) = total hours ≤ H; search [1, max pile].

binary-search-find-minimum-in-rotated-so.title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
binary-search-find-minimum-in-rotated-so.insight: the min is the single inversion point.
binary-search-find-minimum-in-rotated-so.approach: compare `a[mid]` to `a[hi]`; if greater, min is right of mid, else at/left.
binary-search-find-minimum-in-rotated-so.watch: compare against `a[hi]`, not `a[lo]`, to handle the no-rotation case cleanly.

binary-search-split-array-largest-sum.title: Split Array Largest Sum
binary-search-split-array-largest-sum.insight: "minimize the largest subarray sum across k splits" → binary-search that largest-sum value; feasible(x) = the array can be cut into ≤ k pieces each ≤ x.
binary-search-split-array-largest-sum.approach: search [max element, total sum]; greedily count pieces.
binary-search-split-array-largest-sum.watch: this archetype ("minimize the max / maximize the min") is a top-tier FAANG pattern — recognize it instantly.

binary-search-median-of-two-sorted-array.title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
binary-search-median-of-two-sorted-array.insight: binary-search the partition point of the smaller array so left halves of both = right halves in count, with maxLeft ≤ minRight.
binary-search-median-of-two-sorted-array.approach: partition search on the shorter array.
binary-search-median-of-two-sorted-array.watch: the ±∞ sentinels for empty partitions; this is genuinely hard — learn it cold, it's a known filter.

stacks-and-monotonic-stack-valid-parenth.title: Valid Parentheses
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-valid-parenth.insight: a close must match the most recent open.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-valid-parenth.approach: push opens, on a close pop and verify the pair.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-valid-parenth.watch: reject if the stack is empty on a close, or non-empty at the end.

stacks-and-monotonic-stack-min-stack.title: Min Stack
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-min-stack.insight: keep a parallel stack of running minimums.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-min-stack.approach: on push, store `min(value, currentMin)`; pop both together.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-min-stack.watch: the parallel-min stack must push on *every* push, even when min doesn't change.

stacks-and-monotonic-stack-daily-tempera.title: Daily Temperatures
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-daily-tempera.insight: "days until a warmer day" = distance to next-greater.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-daily-tempera.approach: monotonic decreasing stack of indices; on a warmer day, pop and set the gap.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-daily-tempera.watch: store indices to compute the distance.

stacks-and-monotonic-stack-evaluate-reve.title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-evaluate-reve.insight: operands wait on a stack until an operator consumes two.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-evaluate-reve.approach: push numbers, on operator pop two (order matters for `-` and `/`).
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-evaluate-reve.watch: operand order: `b` is popped first, compute `a op b`.

stacks-and-monotonic-stack-generate-pare.title: Generate Parentheses
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-generate-pare.insight: it's backtracking with a validity invariant (opens used ≤ n, closes ≤ opens).
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-generate-pare.approach: recurse adding `(` if available, `)` if it keeps balance.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-generate-pare.watch: listed here because the validity rule is stack-shaped; the algorithm is backtracking (see §9).

stacks-and-monotonic-stack-largest-recta.title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-largest-recta.insight: for each bar, the widest rectangle of its height extends until a shorter bar on each side — exactly previous-smaller and next-smaller.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-largest-recta.approach: monotonic increasing stack; when a shorter bar arrives, pop and compute area `height × width` using the new boundaries.
stacks-and-monotonic-stack-largest-recta.watch: the boss of this pattern; "Maximal Rectangle" (2D) reduces to running this per row.

queues-and-deque-implement-queue-using-s.title: Implement Queue using Stacks
queues-and-deque-implement-queue-using-s.approach: push to `in`; when popping, if `out` empty, drain `in`→`out`.
queues-and-deque-implement-queue-using-s.watch: only transfer when `out` is empty, or you scramble order.

queues-and-deque-design-circular-deque.title: Design Circular Deque
queues-and-deque-design-circular-deque.insight: fixed array + head/tail with modular arithmetic.
queues-and-deque-design-circular-deque.approach: track size; `(idx + cap) % cap` for wraparound.
queues-and-deque-design-circular-deque.watch: distinguishing full vs empty (keep an explicit count).

queues-and-deque-sliding-window-maximum.title: Sliding Window Maximum
queues-and-deque-sliding-window-maximum.watch: it's a queue problem and a window problem; know it from both angles.

linked-lists-reverse-linked-list.title: Reverse Linked List
linked-lists-reverse-linked-list.approach: the template; also know the recursive form.

linked-lists-merge-two-sorted-lists.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
linked-lists-merge-two-sorted-lists.insight: a dummy head removes the special-case for the first node.
linked-lists-merge-two-sorted-lists.approach: splice the smaller head each step; attach the leftover tail.
linked-lists-merge-two-sorted-lists.watch: the dummy node is what keeps this clean.

linked-lists-linked-list-cycle-ii.title: Linked List Cycle II (find cycle start)
linked-lists-linked-list-cycle-ii.insight: after Floyd's meeting, a pointer from head and one from the meeting point advance at equal speed and collide at the cycle entrance.
linked-lists-linked-list-cycle-ii.approach: detect meeting, then reset one to head, step both by 1.
linked-lists-linked-list-cycle-ii.watch: be able to *derive* why the math works (distance algebra), not just recite it — interviewers push on this.

linked-lists-remove-nth-node-from-end.title: Remove Nth Node From End
linked-lists-remove-nth-node-from-end.insight: a gap of n between two pointers locates the target in one pass.
linked-lists-remove-nth-node-from-end.approach: advance `fast` n steps, then move both until `fast` ends; `slow.next` is the node to drop.
linked-lists-remove-nth-node-from-end.watch: use a dummy head so removing the actual head is not a special case.

linked-lists-reorder-list.title: Reorder List
linked-lists-reorder-list.approach: find middle (fast/slow) → reverse second half → merge the two halves alternately.
linked-lists-reorder-list.watch: split cleanly so the first half's tail is nulled.

linked-lists-add-two-numbers.title: Add Two Numbers
linked-lists-add-two-numbers.insight: simulate grade-school addition with a carry.
linked-lists-add-two-numbers.approach: walk both lists, sum + carry, emit digit.
linked-lists-add-two-numbers.watch: the final leftover carry needs an extra node.

linked-lists-lru-cache.title: LRU Cache
linked-lists-lru-cache.insight: O(1) get/put needs hashmap (key→node) + doubly linked list (recency order).
linked-lists-lru-cache.approach: on access, move node to front; on overflow, evict the tail.
linked-lists-lru-cache.watch: a *doubly* linked list (so you can unlink in O(1)); sentinel head+tail nodes kill edge cases. This is also a design favorite.

linked-lists-merge-k-sorted-lists.title: Merge k Sorted Lists
linked-lists-merge-k-sorted-lists.insight: always pull the global minimum head.
linked-lists-merge-k-sorted-lists.approach: min-heap of the k heads (O(n log k)), or divide-and-conquer pairwise merge.
linked-lists-merge-k-sorted-lists.watch: heap comparator on node value; push the popped node's `next`.

linked-lists-reverse-nodes-in-k-group.title: Reverse Nodes in k-Group
linked-lists-reverse-nodes-in-k-group.insight: reverse each block of k, leave a remainder of <k untouched.
linked-lists-reverse-nodes-in-k-group.approach: check k nodes remain, reverse the block, recurse/iterate on the rest, reconnect.
linked-lists-reverse-nodes-in-k-group.watch: the reconnection between reversed groups is where it breaks.

recursion-and-backtracking-subsets.title: Subsets
recursion-and-backtracking-subsets.insight: each element is independently in or out; the recursion tree's every node is an answer.
recursion-and-backtracking-subsets.watch: copy `path` when adding (it's mutated as you backtrack).

recursion-and-backtracking-permutations.title: Permutations
recursion-and-backtracking-permutations.watch: reset `used[i]=false` on the way back up.

recursion-and-backtracking-combination-s.title: Combination Sum
recursion-and-backtracking-combination-s.insight: unlimited reuse → recurse with the *same* start index.
recursion-and-backtracking-combination-s.approach: sort; prune when the remaining target goes negative.
recursion-and-backtracking-combination-s.watch: `i` (not `i+1`) on the recursive call allows reuse; sorting enables early break.

recursion-and-backtracking-word-search.title: Word Search
recursion-and-backtracking-word-search.insight: DFS from each cell, marking the path to avoid reuse.
recursion-and-backtracking-word-search.approach: 4-directional DFS matching the next letter; temporarily overwrite the visited cell, restore on backtrack.
recursion-and-backtracking-word-search.watch: restore the cell after recursion — the classic "mark and unmark."

recursion-and-backtracking-letter-combin.title: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number
recursion-and-backtracking-letter-combin.approach: recurse one digit deep per level.

recursion-and-backtracking-palindrome-pa.title: Palindrome Partitioning
recursion-and-backtracking-palindrome-pa.insight: try every prefix; recurse on the rest only if the prefix is a palindrome.
recursion-and-backtracking-palindrome-pa.approach: backtrack over cut points with a palindrome check.
recursion-and-backtracking-palindrome-pa.watch: precompute palindrome DP to avoid re-checking.

recursion-and-backtracking-n-queens.title: N-Queens
recursion-and-backtracking-n-queens.insight: place one queen per row; track attacked columns and both diagonals as sets.
recursion-and-backtracking-n-queens.approach: recurse row by row; a cell is safe if its column, `r−c`, and `r+c` are all free.
recursion-and-backtracking-n-queens.watch: the two diagonal keys (`r−c` and `r+c`) are the elegant part.

recursion-and-backtracking-sudoku-solver.title: Sudoku Solver
recursion-and-backtracking-sudoku-solver.approach: find an empty cell, try 1–9 that don't violate row/col/box, recurse, undo on failure.
recursion-and-backtracking-sudoku-solver.watch: track row/col/box availability incrementally instead of rescanning.

trees-binary-trees-max-depth-invert-tree.title: Max Depth / Invert Tree / Same Tree
trees-binary-trees-max-depth-invert-tree.watch: these build the reflex; do them until trivial.

trees-binary-trees-level-order-traversal.title: Level Order Traversal
trees-binary-trees-level-order-traversal.insight: freeze `q.Count` at the start of each level to separate levels.
trees-binary-trees-level-order-traversal.watch: the per-level size snapshot is what groups nodes correctly.

trees-binary-trees-diameter-of-binary-tr.title: Diameter of Binary Tree
trees-binary-trees-diameter-of-binary-tr.insight: the longest path through a node = leftDepth + rightDepth; the global answer is the max over all nodes, computed during a depth post-order.
trees-binary-trees-diameter-of-binary-tr.approach: return depth up, side-effect a max.
trees-binary-trees-diameter-of-binary-tr.watch: return depth, but *track* diameter separately — they're different quantities.

trees-binary-trees-lowest-common-ancesto.title: Lowest Common Ancestor (general binary tree)
trees-binary-trees-lowest-common-ancesto.insight: the LCA is the node where the two targets first appear in different subtrees.
trees-binary-trees-lowest-common-ancesto.approach: post-order; if left and right both return non-null, this node is the LCA; else bubble up whichever is non-null.
trees-binary-trees-lowest-common-ancesto.watch: a node is its own ancestor (handle when a target equals the current node).

trees-binary-trees-right-side-view.title: Right Side View
trees-binary-trees-right-side-view.insight: the last node dequeued at each level.
trees-binary-trees-right-side-view.approach: BFS, take the level's final node (or DFS visiting right first, recording first per depth).

trees-binary-trees-construct-from-preord.title: Construct from Preorder + Inorder
trees-binary-trees-construct-from-preord.insight: preorder gives the root; its position in inorder splits left/right subtrees.
trees-binary-trees-construct-from-preord.approach: recurse on index ranges; a value→index map on inorder makes the split O(1).
trees-binary-trees-construct-from-preord.watch: track preorder cursor and inorder bounds carefully.

trees-binary-trees-binary-tree-maximum-p.title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
trees-binary-trees-binary-tree-maximum-p.insight: each node returns the best *straight* downward path to a parent, but the answer may *bend* at a node (left + node + right).
trees-binary-trees-binary-tree-maximum-p.approach: post-order returns `node + max(0, L, R)` upward; update global with `node + max(0,L) + max(0,R)`.
trees-binary-trees-binary-tree-maximum-p.watch: clamp negative contributions to 0; distinguish "return value" from "answer candidate."

trees-binary-trees-serialize-deserialize.title: Serialize/Deserialize
trees-binary-trees-serialize-deserialize.insight: a preorder stream with explicit null markers uniquely encodes a tree.
trees-binary-trees-serialize-deserialize.approach: serialize preorder writing `#` for nulls; deserialize by consuming tokens in the same order.
trees-binary-trees-serialize-deserialize.watch: the null markers are mandatory for unambiguous reconstruction.

binary-search-trees-validate-bst.title: Validate BST
binary-search-trees-validate-bst.insight: local `left<node<right` is *not enough*; every node must lie within an inherited (min,max) range.
binary-search-trees-validate-bst.watch: the classic wrong answer checks only immediate children; use `long` bounds for int-edge values.

binary-search-trees-kth-smallest-in-bst.title: Kth Smallest in BST
binary-search-trees-kth-smallest-in-bst.insight: in-order yields ascending order; stop at the kth.
binary-search-trees-kth-smallest-in-bst.watch: don't traverse the whole tree — stop early.

binary-search-trees-lca-of-bst.title: LCA of BST
binary-search-trees-lca-of-bst.insight: use the ordering — if both targets are smaller go left, both larger go right, else split point = LCA.
binary-search-trees-lca-of-bst.watch: simpler than the general-tree LCA; use the values.

binary-search-trees-insert-delete-in-bst.title: Insert/Delete in BST
binary-search-trees-insert-delete-in-bst.insight: insert at the first null leaf by the ordering; delete's hard case is two children.
binary-search-trees-insert-delete-in-bst.approach: for two-children delete, replace with in-order successor (min of right subtree), then delete that.
binary-search-trees-insert-delete-in-bst.watch: the two-children case is the only real difficulty.

tries-prefix-trees-implement-trie.title: Implement Trie
tries-prefix-trees-implement-trie.approach: insert/search walk the children; `startsWith` is search without requiring `isEnd`.
tries-prefix-trees-implement-trie.watch: the `isEnd` flag distinguishes a stored word from a mere prefix.

tries-prefix-trees-add-and-search-words-.title: Add and Search Words (with `.` wildcard)
tries-prefix-trees-add-and-search-words-.insight: `.` branches into a DFS over all children.
tries-prefix-trees-add-and-search-words-.approach: recursive search; on `.`, try every non-null child.
tries-prefix-trees-add-and-search-words-.watch: the wildcard turns a walk into a search.

tries-prefix-trees-word-search-ii.title: Word Search II
tries-prefix-trees-word-search-ii.insight: build a Trie of the target words, then DFS the grid *guided by the Trie* so you abandon dead prefixes immediately.
tries-prefix-trees-word-search-ii.approach: grid DFS that advances only along existing Trie edges; mark words found.
tries-prefix-trees-word-search-ii.watch: prune found words and dead branches; this Trie+DFS combo is a known hard.

heaps-priority-queues-kth-largest-elemen.title: Kth Largest Element
heaps-priority-queues-kth-largest-elemen.insight: a size-k min-heap keeps exactly the k largest; its root is the answer.
heaps-priority-queues-kth-largest-elemen.approach: template, or quickselect for O(n) average.
heaps-priority-queues-kth-largest-elemen.complexity: O(n log k) heap / O(n) avg quickselect.
heaps-priority-queues-kth-largest-elemen.watch: know quickselect exists — it's the optimal follow-up.

heaps-priority-queues-top-k-frequent-ele.title: Top K Frequent Elements
heaps-priority-queues-top-k-frequent-ele.watch: bucket sort is O(n) and the better answer if asked to optimize.

heaps-priority-queues-task-scheduler.title: Task Scheduler
heaps-priority-queues-task-scheduler.insight: always run the most frequent remaining task to spread out cooldowns.
heaps-priority-queues-task-scheduler.approach: max-heap by count; run up to (n+1) distinct tasks per cycle, decrement, re-add.
heaps-priority-queues-task-scheduler.watch: the closed-form formula `(maxCount-1)*(n+1) + countOfMax` is the elegant alternative.

heaps-priority-queues-k-closest-points-t.title: K Closest Points to Origin
heaps-priority-queues-k-closest-points-t.approach: push, evict the farthest beyond k.

heaps-priority-queues-find-median-from-d.title: Find Median from Data Stream
heaps-priority-queues-find-median-from-d.insight: two heaps — a max-heap for the lower half, a min-heap for the upper — kept balanced; median is the top(s).
heaps-priority-queues-find-median-from-d.approach: push, rebalance so sizes differ by ≤1.
heaps-priority-queues-find-median-from-d.watch: the rebalancing (move a root across when sizes skew) is the crux.

heaps-priority-queues-merge-k-sorted-lis.title: Merge k Sorted Lists

greedy-jump-game.title: Jump Game
greedy-jump-game.insight: track the furthest index reachable so far; you win if it ever reaches the end.
greedy-jump-game.approach: one pass, `reach = max(reach, i + nums[i])`, fail if `i > reach`.
greedy-jump-game.watch: fail fast when an index is unreachable.

greedy-jump-game-ii.title: Jump Game II (min jumps)
greedy-jump-game-ii.insight: greedy BFS by "jump level" — within the current reach, find the furthest next reach.
greedy-jump-game-ii.approach: track current-level end; when you pass it, increment jumps and extend.
greedy-jump-game-ii.watch: this is implicit level-order, not DP.

greedy-gas-station.title: Gas Station
greedy-gas-station.insight: if total gas ≥ total cost, a unique start exists; reset the candidate start whenever the running tank goes negative.
greedy-gas-station.approach: single pass tracking tank and start.
greedy-gas-station.watch: prove why resetting start to `i+1` is safe.

greedy-hand-of-straights.title: Hand of Straights
greedy-hand-of-straights.insight: the smallest available card must begin a group.
greedy-hand-of-straights.approach: counts (sorted/`SortedDictionary`); greedily form consecutive groups from the minimum.
greedy-hand-of-straights.watch: decrement counts and skip exhausted cards.

greedy-candy.title: Candy
greedy-candy.insight: satisfy the left-neighbor and right-neighbor constraints independently, then take the max.
greedy-candy.approach: left-to-right pass (rising ratings get +1), right-to-left pass, combine by max.
greedy-candy.watch: one pass can't satisfy both directions — that's why it's two.

intervals-merge-intervals.title: Merge Intervals
intervals-merge-intervals.insight: sorted by start, overlaps are adjacent.
intervals-merge-intervals.watch: compare `prevEnd >= curStart` for overlap; use max for the merged end.

intervals-insert-interval.title: Insert Interval
intervals-insert-interval.insight: three phases — intervals entirely before, the overlapping run to merge, intervals entirely after.
intervals-insert-interval.approach: copy before, merge the overlapping span, copy after.
intervals-insert-interval.watch: the array is pre-sorted, so no full sort is needed.

intervals-non-overlapping-intervals.title: Non-overlapping Intervals (min removals)
intervals-non-overlapping-intervals.insight: keep as many as possible → greedily keep the interval that ends earliest.
intervals-non-overlapping-intervals.approach: sort by end; count keeps where start ≥ last kept end.

intervals-meeting-rooms-ii.title: Meeting Rooms II (min rooms)
intervals-meeting-rooms-ii.approach: min-heap of end times (reuse a room when the earliest end ≤ next start), or a sweep of +1/−1 events.
intervals-meeting-rooms-ii.watch: the heap holds *end times*; the sweep-line counter is the cleaner mental model.

intervals-employee-free-time.title: Employee Free Time
intervals-employee-free-time.insight: merge all intervals across everyone, then gaps between merged intervals are free time.

graphs-number-of-islands.title: Number of Islands
graphs-number-of-islands.insight: each unvisited land cell starts a flood fill that consumes one island.
graphs-number-of-islands.approach: iterate cells; on land, BFS/DFS marking the whole component.
graphs-number-of-islands.watch: mark visited (mutate or a visited set) to avoid recount.

graphs-clone-graph.title: Clone Graph
graphs-clone-graph.insight: traverse while mapping original→copy so cycles don't loop forever.
graphs-clone-graph.approach: DFS/BFS with a `Dictionary<Node,Node>`; create the clone on first visit.
graphs-clone-graph.watch: check the map *before* recursing into neighbors.

graphs-course-schedule-i-ii.title: Course Schedule I/II
graphs-course-schedule-i-ii.insight: prerequisites form a DAG; a valid order exists iff no cycle, and the order is a topological sort.
graphs-course-schedule-i-ii.watch: "can finish?" = no cycle; "order?" = the topo order itself.

graphs-rotting-oranges.title: Rotting Oranges
graphs-rotting-oranges.insight: multi-source BFS where BFS *levels = minutes elapsed*.
graphs-rotting-oranges.approach: enqueue all rotten cells at level 0, BFS outward, count levels.
graphs-rotting-oranges.watch: seed *all* sources before starting; check for unreachable fresh oranges at the end.

graphs-pacific-atlantic-water-flow.title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
graphs-pacific-atlantic-water-flow.insight: instead of "can this cell reach both oceans," reverse it — BFS *inland from each ocean's border*; the intersection reaches both.
graphs-pacific-atlantic-water-flow.approach: two multi-source BFS/DFS from the two border sets, intersect.
graphs-pacific-atlantic-water-flow.watch: reversing the flow direction is the unlock.

graphs-number-of-connected-components-gr.title: Number of Connected Components / Graph Valid Tree
graphs-number-of-connected-components-gr.insight: Union-Find counts components; a tree has exactly n−1 edges and no cycle.
graphs-number-of-connected-components-gr.approach: union each edge; a `Union` returning false (already connected) means a cycle.
graphs-number-of-connected-components-gr.watch: for "valid tree," also verify edge count = n−1.

graphs-word-ladder.title: Word Ladder
graphs-word-ladder.insight: words are nodes; an edge connects words differing by one letter; shortest transformation = BFS.
graphs-word-ladder.approach: BFS, generating neighbors by trying each position × 26 letters.
graphs-word-ladder.watch: precompute or generate neighbors efficiently; bidirectional BFS is the optimization.

graphs-network-delay-time.title: Network Delay Time
graphs-network-delay-time.insight: shortest time to reach all = max over single-source shortest paths → Dijkstra.
graphs-network-delay-time.approach: Dijkstra template, answer = max finite dist (∞ ⇒ unreachable).

graphs-cheapest-flights-within-k-stops.title: Cheapest Flights Within K Stops
graphs-cheapest-flights-within-k-stops.insight: shortest path with a *hop limit* → Bellman-Ford run K+1 times (each round relaxes one more hop), or Dijkstra with (cost, stops) state.
graphs-cheapest-flights-within-k-stops.approach: Bellman-Ford on a *snapshot* of distances per round so you don't use >K stops in one round.
graphs-cheapest-flights-within-k-stops.watch: relax from a frozen copy each round, or you'll overcount hops.

graphs-alien-dictionary.title: Alien Dictionary
graphs-alien-dictionary.insight: adjacent words reveal one ordering edge at their first differing char; the alphabet order is a topological sort.
graphs-alien-dictionary.approach: build the precedence graph from adjacent pairs, Kahn's.
graphs-alien-dictionary.watch: the prefix edge case (`"abc"` before `"ab"` is invalid) and cycle ⇒ no valid order.

graphs-min-cost-to-connect-all-points.title: Min Cost to Connect All Points
graphs-min-cost-to-connect-all-points.insight: connect all nodes at minimum total edge weight = Minimum Spanning Tree.
graphs-min-cost-to-connect-all-points.approach: Prim (heap, grow the tree) or Kruskal (sort edges + Union-Find).
graphs-min-cost-to-connect-all-points.complexity: Prim O(n² )/O(E log V); Kruskal O(E log E).
graphs-min-cost-to-connect-all-points.watch: on a dense complete graph (all pairs), Prim with an array is competitive.

dynamic-programming-climbing-stairs.title: Climbing Stairs
dynamic-programming-climbing-stairs.watch: this is your DP "hello world"; internalize state+transition here.

dynamic-programming-house-robber-i.title: House Robber I
dynamic-programming-house-robber-i.insight: at each house, either skip it (keep `dp[i-1]`) or rob it (`dp[i-2] + value`).
dynamic-programming-house-robber-i.approach: `dp[i]` = max loot through house i. `max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]+a[i])`.

dynamic-programming-house-robber-ii.title: House Robber II (circular)
dynamic-programming-house-robber-ii.insight: first and last are now adjacent → run the linear version twice, excluding one end each time.
dynamic-programming-house-robber-ii.watch: the circularity is handled by two linear runs, not new logic.

dynamic-programming-decode-ways.title: Decode Ways
dynamic-programming-decode-ways.insight: like stairs but transitions are conditional on valid 1- and 2-digit codes.
dynamic-programming-decode-ways.watch: zeros are the trap (`"0"` decodes to nothing; `"10"`/`"20"` only as pairs).

dynamic-programming-coin-change.title: Coin Change (min coins)
dynamic-programming-coin-change.watch: unreachable = sentinel; coins reusable ⇒ inner loop over coins, amount ascending.

dynamic-programming-coin-change-ii.title: Coin Change II (count ways)
dynamic-programming-coin-change-ii.insight: loop coins *outer*, amount inner, to count combinations not permutations.
dynamic-programming-coin-change-ii.watch: loop order determines combinations vs permutations — a classic subtlety.

dynamic-programming-partition-equal-subs.title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
dynamic-programming-partition-equal-subs.insight: "can a subset sum to total/2?" = 0/1 subset-sum.

dynamic-programming-target-sum.title: Target Sum
dynamic-programming-target-sum.insight: assigning ± signs to reach T reduces to a subset-sum (`P − N = T`, `P + N = total`).
dynamic-programming-target-sum.watch: the algebraic reduction to subset sum is the trick.

dynamic-programming-longest-increasing-s.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
dynamic-programming-longest-increasing-s.insight: `dp[i]` = LIS ending at i = 1 + max dp[j] for j<i with a[j]<a[i].
dynamic-programming-longest-increasing-s.watch: the O(n log n) "patience" version maintains tails via binary search — learn it; it's expected at FAANG.

dynamic-programming-longest-common-subse.title: Longest Common Subsequence
dynamic-programming-longest-common-subse.approach: `dp[i][j]` = LCS of prefixes. match ⇒ `dp[i-1][j-1]+1`, else `max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])`.
dynamic-programming-longest-common-subse.watch: this grid recurrence underlies edit distance, diff tools, etc.

dynamic-programming-word-break.title: Word Break
dynamic-programming-word-break.insight: `dp[i]` = prefix of length i is segmentable, if some `dp[j]` true and `s[j..i]` in dict.
dynamic-programming-word-break.watch: a Trie or hashset for the dictionary keeps the inner check cheap.

dynamic-programming-maximum-product-suba.title: Maximum Product Subarray
dynamic-programming-maximum-product-suba.insight: a negative flips min↔max, so track *both* running max and min.
dynamic-programming-maximum-product-suba.watch: swap max/min when the current number is negative.

dynamic-programming-longest-palindromic-.title: Longest Palindromic Substring
dynamic-programming-longest-palindromic-.insight: expand around each of the 2n−1 centers, *or* `dp[i][j]` = is s[i..j] a palindrome.
dynamic-programming-longest-palindromic-.watch: expand-around-center is O(1) space and simpler to code.

dynamic-programming-unique-paths-min-pat.title: Unique Paths / Min Path Sum
dynamic-programming-unique-paths-min-pat.insight: `dp[i][j]` combines the cell above and to the left.
dynamic-programming-unique-paths-min-pat.complexity: O(mn), O(n) with a rolling row.

dynamic-programming-edit-distance.title: Edit Distance
dynamic-programming-edit-distance.insight: `dp[i][j]` = min edits to turn prefix i into prefix j; choices = insert/delete/replace.
dynamic-programming-edit-distance.approach: match ⇒ diagonal; else `1 + min(insert, delete, replace)`.
dynamic-programming-edit-distance.watch: the three-way min and base cases (empty string ⇒ length).

dynamic-programming-burst-balloons.title: Burst Balloons
dynamic-programming-burst-balloons.insight: think of which balloon is burst *last* in a range; that fixes the multiplication and splits the range.
dynamic-programming-burst-balloons.approach: `dp[i][j]` = max coins bursting the open interval (i,j).
dynamic-programming-burst-balloons.watch: "last to burst," not first — counterintuitive but it's what makes the subproblems independent.

dynamic-programming-regular-expression-m.title: Regular Expression Matching
dynamic-programming-regular-expression-m.insight: 2D DP over text/pattern; `*` means "zero of the preceding" or "one more of the preceding."
dynamic-programming-regular-expression-m.watch: the `*` transition (two branches) and `.` are the whole difficulty.

dynamic-programming-best-time-to-buy-sel.title: Best Time to Buy/Sell Stock III & IV (≤k transactions)
dynamic-programming-best-time-to-buy-sel.approach: track buy/sell profits per allowed transaction.
dynamic-programming-best-time-to-buy-sel.watch: when k ≥ n/2 it degenerates to unlimited transactions (greedy) — handle that to avoid memory blowup.

dynamic-programming-house-robber-iii.title: House Robber III
dynamic-programming-house-robber-iii.insight: each node returns two values: best-if-robbed and best-if-skipped.
dynamic-programming-house-robber-iii.watch: returning a tuple up the tree is the tree-DP signature.

strings-specialized-pattern-matching-imp.title: Implement strStr()
strings-specialized-pattern-matching-imp.insight: substring search; KMP or rolling hash for linear time.
strings-specialized-pattern-matching-imp.watch: naive O(nm) is accepted but the linear method is the "strong" answer.

strings-specialized-pattern-matching-rep.title: Repeated Substring Pattern
strings-specialized-pattern-matching-rep.insight: the KMP failure value of the whole string reveals the smallest repeating unit.
strings-specialized-pattern-matching-rep.watch: the slick check: `s` is repeating iff it's a non-trivial rotation of itself — `(s+s).IndexOf(s, 1) < s.Length`.

strings-specialized-pattern-matching-sho.title: Shortest Palindrome
strings-specialized-pattern-matching-sho.insight: find the longest palindromic *prefix* via KMP on `s + '#' + reverse(s)`.
strings-specialized-pattern-matching-sho.watch: the separator `#` prevents overcounting across the join.

advanced-structures-faang-hard-awareness.title: Range Sum Query — Mutable
advanced-structures-faang-hard-awareness.insight: updates + range sums → Fenwick or segment tree.
advanced-structures-faang-hard-awareness.watch: Fenwick is the cleaner choice for pure sums.

advanced-structures-faang-hard-awareness.title: Count of Smaller Numbers After Self
advanced-structures-faang-hard-awareness.insight: process right-to-left, querying "how many already-seen values are smaller" → BIT over value-ranks, or a modified merge sort that counts inversions.
advanced-structures-faang-hard-awareness.watch: coordinate-compress values first for the BIT.

math-and-geometry-occasional-awareness-h.title: Happy Number
math-and-geometry-occasional-awareness-h.insight: iterating digit-square-sums either reaches 1 or cycles → Floyd's cycle detection (fast/slow on the transformation).
math-and-geometry-occasional-awareness-h.watch: reuse the linked-list cycle trick on a numeric sequence.

math-and-geometry-occasional-awareness-r.title: Rotate Image (90°, in place)
math-and-geometry-occasional-awareness-r.watch: the transpose+reverse decomposition avoids fiddly index math.

math-and-geometry-occasional-awareness-s.title: Spiral Matrix
math-and-geometry-occasional-awareness-s.approach: traverse each edge, then contract that boundary.
math-and-geometry-occasional-awareness-s.watch: re-check boundaries before the bottom and left passes to avoid double-visiting on non-square grids.

design-0-what-a-system-design-interview-.title: What a system design interview actually tests — overview
design-0-what-a-system-design-interview-.body:
Junior candidates list components. Senior candidates make **defensible tradeoffs under constraints**. The interviewer is grading five things:

1. **Requirements & scoping** — do you nail down what to build before building it?
2. **Estimation** — can you size the problem (QPS, storage, bandwidth) and let the numbers drive decisions?
3. **High-level structure** — clean data flow, sensible component boundaries.
4. **Deep dives** — when pushed on the hard part (the bottleneck), do you go deep with real mechanisms?
5. **Tradeoff articulation** — every choice has a cost; do you name it and justify it? *This is where seniority is won or lost.*

The fatal mistake at your level: jumping to a diagram before clarifying requirements, or naming "Kafka" / "Redis" without saying *why* and *what it costs*. Drive with the numbers and the tradeoffs.

design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-1-1.title: The estimation toolkit — overview
design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-1-1.body: You can't reason about scale you can't size. Memorize these.

design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-latency-.title: Latency numbers (orders of magnitude — say "roughly")
design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-latency-.body:
| Operation | Time |
|---|---|
| L1 cache reference | ~1 ns |
| Branch mispredict | ~3 ns |
| Mutex lock/unlock | ~20 ns |
| Main memory reference | ~100 ns |
| Compress 1 KB (Snappy) | ~2 µs |
| Send 1 KB over 1 Gbps network | ~10 µs |
| SSD random read | ~100 µs |
| Round trip within a datacenter | ~0.5 ms |
| Read 1 MB sequentially from SSD | ~1 ms |
| HDD seek | ~10 ms |
| Round trip across the world (e.g. US↔EU) | ~150 ms |

**The takeaways that drive design:** memory is ~1000× faster than SSD, SSD ~100× faster than spinning disk, and a cross-region round trip costs ~150 ms — which is why you cache aggressively, keep hot data in RAM, and put compute near data and users (CDN/edge).

design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-powers-o.title: Powers of two & data sizes
design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-powers-o.body:
- 2¹⁰ ≈ 1 thousand (KB), 2²⁰ ≈ 1 million (MB), 2³⁰ ≈ 1 billion (GB), 2⁴⁰ ≈ 1 trillion (TB).
- ASCII char = 1 byte; UTF-8 = 1–4; `long`/`double` = 8 bytes; a UUID = 16 bytes; a typical row with a few fields = ~hundreds of bytes.

design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-the-qps-.title: The QPS shortcut
design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-the-qps-.body:
A day has ~86,400 seconds ≈ **10⁵**. So:
- **1 million requests/day ≈ ~12 QPS** (1M / 10⁵).
- **100 million/day ≈ ~1,200 QPS.**
- **1 billion/day ≈ ~12,000 QPS.**
- **Peak** is typically **2–3× average** — size for peak.

design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-worked-e.title: Worked example (do this out loud in interviews)
design-1-the-estimation-toolkit-worked-e.body:
*"Design a URL shortener handling 100M new URLs/month."*
- Writes: 100M/month ÷ (30 × 10⁵ s) ≈ **~40 writes/s** average, ~120/s peak.
- Reads: assume 100:1 read:write ratio → **~4,000 reads/s** average. *This tells you it's a read-heavy system → cache the redirects.*
- Storage: 100M/month × 12 months × 5 years × ~500 bytes/record ≈ 6B records × 500 B ≈ **~3 TB over 5 years.** *Fits comfortably; not a storage-bound problem.*
- The numbers just told you: read-heavy, cache-first, storage is trivial, the short-code generation + low-latency redirect is the real design problem.

That move — letting back-of-envelope numbers *select the hard problem* — is the senior signal.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Scaling: vertical vs horizontal, and the stateless prerequisite
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
- **Vertical** (bigger machine): simple, but a ceiling and a single point of failure.
- **Horizontal** (more machines): the real answer for scale, but requires your services to be **stateless** — no per-request state stored in process memory — so any instance can serve any request behind a load balancer. Push state to a shared store (DB, Redis).
- *Your anchor:* your multi-pod K8s session-sharing fix is exactly this — moving session state out of pod memory into distributed Redis so pods became stateless and horizontally scalable. That's the textbook lesson, lived.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Load balancers
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
- **L4 (transport):** routes by IP/port, doesn't inspect content. Fast, protocol-agnostic.
- **L7 (application):** inspects HTTP — can route by path/header/cookie, do TLS termination, sticky sessions. More capable, slightly more overhead. *Your YARP proxy is an L7 reverse proxy* — own that distinction.
- **Algorithms:** round-robin, weighted, least-connections, least-latency, and **consistent hashing** (critical when backends hold state/cache — minimizes reshuffling when a node joins/leaves).
- **Health checks** remove dead backends; LBs are themselves made HA in pairs.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Caching (know this cold — it's the most-asked block)
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
**Why:** absorb read load and cut latency by keeping hot data close (RAM, edge).

**Strategies:**
- **Cache-aside (lazy):** app checks cache; on miss, reads DB and populates cache. Most common. Risk: stale data, and a cold cache thundering the DB.
- **Read-through:** the cache layer itself loads from DB on miss (app only talks to cache).
- **Write-through:** writes go to cache *and* DB synchronously — consistent but slower writes.
- **Write-back (write-behind):** write to cache, flush to DB async — fast but risks data loss on crash.

**Eviction:** LRU (most common), LFU, FIFO, TTL-based. Size + eviction policy determine hit rate.

**The two hard problems:**
- **Cache stampede / thundering herd:** a hot key expires and thousands of concurrent requests all miss and hammer the DB simultaneously. Fixes: a **lock/semaphore so only one request recomputes** while others wait or serve stale (*your exact fix — semaphore + IMemoryCache*), probabilistic early expiration, or request coalescing. This is a gold interview story for you — tell it.
- **Cache invalidation:** "the second hard thing in CS." Strategies: TTL (simple, eventual staleness), write-through (consistent, costly), explicit invalidation on write (precise, complex), versioned keys. Name the staleness tolerance the use case allows.

**Where caches live:** client → CDN/edge → load balancer → application (in-process or Redis/Memcached) → database (buffer pool). Each layer absorbs load before the next.

**Redis vs Memcached:** Memcached is a simple in-memory KV cache; Redis adds rich data structures (sorted sets, hashes, streams), persistence, pub/sub, Lua, and clustering. You run Redis — be ready to discuss the 24 MB-key memory blowup (large values fragment memory and cause eviction pressure / OOM in constrained pods) as a real operational lesson.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: CDN
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body: Geographically distributed edge servers cache static (and increasingly dynamic) content near users, cutting latency and origin load. Pull (lazy fill on first request) vs push (you upload). Use for images/video/JS/CSS and cacheable API responses. The win is the ~150 ms cross-region round trip you *avoid*.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Databases — the heart of most designs
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
**SQL vs NoSQL — choose on the real axes, not hype:**
- **Relational (Postgres/MySQL):** structured schema, **ACID transactions**, joins, strong consistency. Best when relationships and integrity matter and you need ad-hoc queries. Scales vertically easily, horizontally with effort (sharding/read replicas). *Your DigitalReceipt is here.*
- **NoSQL families:**
  - **Key-value (Redis, DynamoDB):** O(1) lookups by key, massive scale, simple model.
  - **Document (MongoDB):** flexible JSON docs, good when the access pattern is "fetch this whole object."
  - **Wide-column (Cassandra, HBase):** write-optimized, linear horizontal scale, tunable consistency — built for huge write throughput and time-series.
  - **Graph (Neo4j):** relationship-heavy traversals (social graphs).
- **The honest framing:** NoSQL trades joins/transactions/flexibility-of-query for horizontal scale and write throughput. Pick by *access pattern*: model the queries first, then the schema. "We need ad-hoc reporting and integrity → relational. We need 100k writes/s of time-series with known access patterns → wide-column."

**Indexing internals (a likely deep-dive):**
- **B-tree / B+-tree** (the relational default): balanced, high fanout so the tree is shallow (3–4 levels for billions of rows), each node ≈ a disk page → few disk reads per lookup. Great for reads and range scans. Updates cost (rebalancing, write amplification). *Your partition pruning + index propagation work lives at this layer — pruning lets the planner skip whole partitions' B-trees.*
- **LSM-tree** (Cassandra, RocksDB, modern write-heavy stores): buffers writes in memory (memtable), flushes sorted runs to disk (SSTables), merges them via compaction. **Write-optimized** (sequential writes) at the cost of read amplification (may check several SSTables; mitigated by bloom filters). The B-tree-vs-LSM tradeoff = read-optimized vs write-optimized; name it.
- **Practical index rules:** composite-index column order matters (leftmost-prefix); covering indexes serve a query entirely from the index; over-indexing slows writes and bloats storage; an index on a low-cardinality column is often useless.

**Replication:**
- **Leader–follower (primary–replica):** writes to the leader, reads can fan out to followers (scales reads). **Replication lag** means followers can serve stale reads → **read-your-own-writes** problems. Sync replication = consistent but slower/availability-risk; async = fast but can lose recent writes on leader failure.
- **Multi-leader / leaderless (Dynamo-style quorums):** higher availability, but write conflicts need resolution (last-write-wins, vector clocks, CRDTs).
- **Quorum math:** with N replicas, if **W + R > N** (write quorum + read quorum), a read is guaranteed to see the latest write. Tune W/R for consistency-vs-latency.

**Partitioning / sharding (you live this with 120+ tenant schemas):**
- **Why:** when data or write throughput exceeds one machine, split it.
- **Strategies:** **range** (by key range — good for range queries, risks hotspots), **hash** (even distribution, loses range queries), **directory/lookup** (a mapping service — flexible, adds a hop), and **by tenant** (your model — natural isolation, but uneven tenant sizes create hot shards).
- **The hard parts:** rebalancing when adding shards (consistent hashing minimizes movement), cross-shard queries and joins (avoid or fan-out-and-merge), cross-shard transactions (avoid — they're slow and complex), and **hotspots** (a celebrity user / huge tenant). Discuss how you'd detect and split a hot shard.

**Transactions & isolation (deep-dive bait — see Part 3 for full depth):** ACID, isolation levels (read committed → repeatable read → serializable) and the anomalies each prevents, and MVCC (how Postgres gives snapshot isolation without read locks — and the VACUUM cost you've felt). Know that distributed transactions across shards/services are the thing you *design around*, not toward.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Consistency models & CAP
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
- **CAP:** during a **network partition**, you must choose **Consistency** (reject/stall to avoid stale reads) or **Availability** (serve possibly-stale data). It's only a forced choice *during a partition*.
- **PACELC** completes it: **else** (no partition), you still trade **Latency vs Consistency**. A more honest framing than CAP alone — say it and you sound senior.
- **The consistency spectrum:** **strong/linearizable** (everyone sees the latest write immediately — expensive, needs coordination) → **sequential** → **causal** (causally-related ops seen in order) → **read-your-writes** → **eventual** (replicas converge eventually). Pick the *weakest* model the use case tolerates — strong consistency is a tax. A bank balance wants strong; a like-count tolerates eventual.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Message queues, streaming & async (you run RabbitMQ — own this)
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
**Why async:** decouple producers from consumers, absorb spikes (buffering/backpressure), smooth load, enable retries and fan-out. Turns a slow synchronous chain into a resilient pipeline.

- **Queue (RabbitMQ, SQS):** work distribution — each message consumed by one worker. Good for task pipelines (your data-pipeline workers).
- **Pub/Sub:** one message → many subscribers (fan-out).
- **Log (Kafka):** an append-only, partitioned, *replayable* log; consumers track their own offset. Built for high-throughput streaming, event sourcing, and multiple independent consumers reading the same stream. **RabbitMQ vs Kafka:** RabbitMQ = flexible routing, per-message ack, good for task queues; Kafka = ordered partitions, retention/replay, massive throughput, good for event streams. Know when each fits.

**Delivery semantics (must-know):**
- **At-most-once:** may lose messages (fire and forget).
- **At-least-once:** never lost, but may **duplicate** (redelivery on failed ack) — the common default.
- **Exactly-once:** the holy grail, and *practically a lie* end-to-end. You achieve effective exactly-once with **at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumers** (dedupe by message ID / idempotency key). Say this — it's a senior tell.

**Related patterns:**
- **Idempotency:** processing the same message twice yields the same result. Implement with idempotency keys, dedupe tables, or naturally-idempotent operations (upserts).
- **Dead-letter queue (DLQ):** messages that repeatedly fail go here for inspection instead of blocking the queue.
- **Backpressure:** when consumers fall behind, signal producers to slow down (or the queue buffers/sheds).
- **Ordering:** global ordering is expensive; partition-level ordering (Kafka per-partition) is the usual compromise — partition by a key (e.g. userId) to keep per-key order.
- **The dual-write / outbox problem:** you can't atomically write to a DB *and* publish to a queue (two systems, no shared transaction). Solution: the **transactional outbox** — write the event into an `outbox` table in the *same* DB transaction, then a separate process (or CDC) publishes it. This guarantees the event fires iff the DB write committed. (Ties directly to your RabbitMQ pipelines.)

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Consensus & coordination (awareness → Part 3 depth)
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body: When multiple nodes must agree (who's the leader, is this committed), you need **consensus**. **Raft** is the one to understand: a leader is elected by majority vote; the leader appends entries to a replicated log; an entry commits once a majority has it. Used by etcd, Consul, etc., for leader election and config. You rarely implement it — you *use* systems built on it (Kubernetes' control plane uses etcd/Raft). Know *why* it exists: to get agreement despite failures, without a single point of truth.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Rate limiting (common standalone question and component)
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
- **Token bucket:** tokens refill at a rate; each request consumes one; burst-friendly up to bucket size. The usual default.
- **Leaky bucket:** processes at a fixed rate, smoothing bursts (a queue draining steadily).
- **Fixed window counter:** count per time window — simple but allows 2× bursts at window edges.
- **Sliding window log / counter:** smooths the edge problem; sliding-window-counter is the practical balance.
- **Distributed rate limiting:** the counter must be shared → Redis (atomic INCR + TTL, or a Lua script for atomicity). *You've built rate limiting — design it cleanly with token bucket in Redis.*

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: API design
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
- **REST:** resource-oriented, HTTP verbs, stateless, cacheable. Default for public/CRUD APIs. Know idempotent methods (GET/PUT/DELETE idempotent; POST not) and **idempotency keys** for safe retries on POST (payments!).
- **gRPC:** binary (Protobuf), HTTP/2, streaming, low latency, strongly-typed contracts. Best for **internal service-to-service** comms.
- **GraphQL:** client specifies exactly the fields it needs — solves over/under-fetching for complex, varied clients; cost: caching is harder, query complexity must be bounded.
- **Pagination:** offset (simple, slow + inconsistent at scale) vs **cursor/keyset** (stable, efficient — paginate by `WHERE id > last_seen`). Use cursor for large/changing datasets.

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Search (you run Elasticsearch — a free deep-dive)
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body: Full-text search uses an **inverted index**: a map from each term → the list of documents containing it (a posting list). Querying intersects/unions posting lists, then ranks (TF-IDF / BM25). Elasticsearch shards the index for scale and replicates for availability. Use it when you need relevance-ranked text search, not exact lookups (that's what your DB does). Be ready to contrast: a B-tree index answers "rows where col = X"; an inverted index answers "documents *about* X, ranked."

design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.title: Observability & reliability
design-2-the-building-blocks-in-depth-2-.body:
- **The three pillars:** **logs** (discrete events), **metrics** (aggregated numbers/time-series — counters, gauges, histograms), **traces** (a request's path across services). *Your YARP observability layer = a metrics/tracing story.*
- **SLI/SLO/SLA:** SLI = the measured indicator (p99 latency), SLO = your internal target (p99 < 200ms 99.9% of the time), SLA = the contractual promise with penalties. Design to the SLO.
- **Reliability patterns:**
  - **Timeouts** — never wait forever on a dependency.
  - **Retries with exponential backoff + jitter** — retry transient failures, but back off and randomize to avoid retry storms synchronizing.
  - **Circuit breaker** — after N failures, "open" the circuit and fail fast for a cooldown, giving the dependency room to recover (Polly in .NET).
  - **Bulkhead** — isolate resource pools so one failing dependency can't exhaust all threads/connections.
  - **Graceful degradation** — serve stale cache / a reduced experience instead of erroring (e.g. show cached feed if the ranking service is down).
  - **Idempotency + dead-letter** for async paths.

design-3-the-interview-framework-run-thi.title: The interview framework (run this every time) — overview
design-3-the-interview-framework-run-thi.body:
**Step 1 — Clarify & scope (don't skip; ~5 min).** Functional requirements (what it does) and non-functional (scale, latency, consistency, availability). Cut scope explicitly: "I'll focus on posting and the feed, and leave out DMs." Get the interviewer to confirm.

**Step 2 — Estimate.** QPS (read vs write), storage, bandwidth. Use the toolkit above. Let the numbers flag the hard problem (read-heavy? write-heavy? storage-bound?).

**Step 3 — API contract.** Define the few core endpoints (request/response). Concrete signatures anchor the rest of the discussion.

**Step 4 — High-level design.** Draw the data flow: client → LB → services → data stores → async workers. Keep services stateless. Name the datastore per component.

**Step 5 — Data model.** Schema + storage choice + *why*. Access patterns drive this. Mention partitioning/sharding key.

**Step 6 — Deep dive.** The interviewer steers to the hard part (the feed fan-out, the dedupe, the geo-index). Go deep with real mechanisms. This is most of the interview.

**Step 7 — Scale, bottlenecks, tradeoffs.** Where does it break? (hot keys, single points of failure, the DB.) Add caching, replication, sharding, queues — and *name what each costs*. Discuss failure modes and reliability patterns. End on tradeoffs.

What "good" looks like: you drive, you quantify, you justify each choice and name its cost, and when pushed you have a real mechanism — not a logo.

design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-1-19.title: URL Shortener (the warm-up that tests fundamentals)
design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-1-19.body:
**Scope:** shorten a long URL → short code; redirect short→long. Custom aliases optional; analytics optional.
**Numbers:** (from §1) ~40 writes/s, ~4,000 reads/s, ~3 TB/5yr. **Read-heavy, cache-first, storage trivial.**
**API:** `POST /shorten {url} → {shortCode}`; `GET /{shortCode} → 302 redirect`.
**Short code generation — the core problem:**
- Base62 (`[A-Za-z0-9]`) encoding of a unique ID. 62⁷ ≈ 3.5 trillion codes in 7 chars — plenty.
- *Option A — counter + base62:* a distributed counter (or DB auto-increment, or a range-allocator giving each app server a block of IDs to avoid a per-write coordination hop). Encode the integer to base62. Simple, no collisions, but codes are sequential/guessable.
- *Option B — hash (e.g. MD5/SHA of URL) + take first 7 chars:* risk of collisions → check-and-retry. Handles dedup of identical URLs naturally.
- Choose A with range-allocation for collision-free, low-coordination generation.
**Data model:** KV is ideal — `shortCode → longURL`. A KV store (DynamoDB) or a sharded relational table keyed by shortCode.
**Read path (the hot path):** `GET /{code}` → check **Redis cache** → on miss, DB lookup, populate cache → `302`. Cache hit rate will be high (popular links dominate). CDN can even cache the redirect at the edge.
**Scale/tradeoffs:** read-heavy → cache + read replicas; storage is trivial so no sharding pressure early, but shard by shortCode hash when needed; analytics go through an **async queue** (don't block the redirect — fire an event, aggregate offline). Custom aliases → check uniqueness on write.

design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-2-20.title: Distributed Rate Limiter
design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-2-20.body:
**Scope:** limit each user/API-key to N requests per window, across many app servers.
**Algorithm:** token bucket (burst-friendly) — each key has a bucket of tokens refilling at rate r.
**Distributed state:** the bucket must be shared across servers → **Redis**. Store `tokens` and `lastRefill` per key. On each request, run a **Lua script** (atomic) that: computes tokens refilled since lastRefill, adds them (capped at bucket size), and if ≥1, decrements and allows; else denies. Atomicity via Lua avoids race conditions between read and write.
**Tradeoffs:** Redis is now in the hot path → keep it co-located/low-latency, and **fail open or closed** deliberately (if Redis is down, do you allow all traffic or block it? Usually fail-open for availability, with a local fallback limiter). Sliding-window-counter is the alternative if you want smoother edges. Sharding the limiter by key spreads Redis load.

design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-3-21.title: News Feed / Twitter (the fan-out classic)
design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-3-21.body:
**Scope:** users post; users see a feed of posts from people they follow, newest-first.
**Numbers:** assume 100M DAU, ~2 posts/user/day → ~2,300 writes/s; feed reads far higher → read-heavy, but writes fan out massively.
**The core tension — fan-out on write vs read:**
- **Fan-out on write (push):** when you post, immediately write the post ID into the precomputed feed cache of every follower. **Feed reads are O(1)** (just read your cache). Cost: a celebrity with 50M followers triggers 50M writes per post — the **"hot user" problem.**
- **Fan-out on read (pull):** store posts once; when a user opens their feed, fetch recent posts from everyone they follow and merge. Cheap writes, but **expensive reads** (fan-in across thousands of followees).
- **The hybrid (the senior answer):** push for normal users; for celebrities, *don't* fan out — instead, at read time, merge the (cached) push-feed with a pull of the few celebrities the user follows. Best of both.
**Data model:** posts in a store keyed by postId (sharded by postId or userId); the social graph (follower/followee) in its own store; per-user feed = a cached list of post IDs (Redis sorted set by timestamp).
**Read path:** read the user's feed (post IDs from Redis) → hydrate posts (batch fetch, cache) → merge celebrity posts → return. Pagination via cursor (timestamp/ID).
**Scale/tradeoffs:** the feed cache (Redis) absorbs reads; fan-out workers run async via a **queue** (posting returns immediately, fan-out happens in the background); ranking (if not pure chronological) adds an async scoring service. Eventual consistency is fine here — a post appearing a second late is acceptable (name that tolerance).

design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-4-22.title: Chat / Messaging (WhatsApp-lite)
design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-4-22.body:
**Scope:** 1:1 messaging, delivery + read receipts, online presence, message history.
**Core challenge:** real-time, bidirectional delivery → **persistent connections** (WebSocket), not request/response polling.
**Design:** clients hold a WebSocket to a **connection/gateway service**. Because users connect to *different* gateway servers, you need a way to route a message to the server holding the recipient's connection: a **presence/routing registry** (Redis: `userId → gatewayServerId`). Message flow: sender → their gateway → lookup recipient's gateway via registry → forward → recipient's gateway pushes over its WebSocket. If the recipient is offline, persist and push via mobile notification; deliver on reconnect.
**Storage:** messages in a write-heavy, time-ordered store — **wide-column (Cassandra)** keyed by `(conversationId, timestamp)` fits the access pattern (recent messages per conversation, huge write volume). 
**Delivery guarantees:** at-least-once + message IDs for client-side dedup; ack-based delivery/read receipts; ordering per conversation via timestamps/sequence numbers.
**Scale/tradeoffs:** millions of concurrent WebSockets → many gateway servers, the registry is the coordination point (keep it fast, replicated). Presence is high-churn → eventual consistency and TTL-based heartbeats. Group chat = fan-out to N members (push to each connected member's gateway).

design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-5-23.title: Notification System (multi-channel)
design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-5-23.body:
**Scope:** send notifications via email / SMS / push, at scale, reliably, without spamming.
**Design:** a **notification service** receives events → applies user preferences & rate limits / dedup → enqueues per-channel jobs onto a **message queue** → channel-specific **workers** (email worker, SMS worker, push worker) call third-party providers (SES, Twilio, FCM). 
**Reliability:** the queue gives retries + buffering; failed sends → **DLQ**; **idempotency keys** prevent duplicate sends on retry (don't email someone twice because a worker crashed after sending but before acking). Provider rate limits → token-bucket per provider. 
**Tradeoffs:** template rendering and personalization as a separate concern; user preference checks and quiet-hours upfront; analytics (delivered/opened) via an async event stream. This is a near-perfect showcase for *your* RabbitMQ pipeline experience — frame it in those terms.

design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-6-24.title: Distributed Cache (design Redis-as-a-service — leans on your strength)
design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-6-24.body:
**Scope:** a horizontally-scalable in-memory KV cache with get/put/TTL.
**Design:** partition the keyspace across N cache nodes via **consistent hashing** (so adding/removing a node only remaps ~1/N of keys, not everything). A client library (or a proxy) hashes the key to find its node. 
**Replication:** each shard has a primary + replica(s) for availability; reads can hit replicas (eventual consistency) or primary (strong). 
**Eviction:** LRU per node when memory is full; TTLs for staleness control. 
**The hard parts (your operational scars apply):** **hot keys** (one key getting disproportionate traffic — mitigate with client-side caching or key replication/splitting), **large values** (your 24 MB-key lesson — big values fragment memory and cause eviction pressure; consider value-size limits and chunking), **thundering herd** on expiry (request coalescing — your semaphore fix), and **cache coherence** with the backing DB (TTL vs write-through vs explicit invalidation). 
**Tradeoffs:** consistency vs availability on replica reads; memory cost vs hit rate; eviction policy vs workload.

design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-7-25.title: The meta-design — your own multi-tenant SaaS platform
design-4-fully-worked-designs-4-7-25.body: The single best prep you have: **be able to whiteboard DigitalReceipt crisply.** Structure it as: multi-tenancy strategy (schema-per-tenant — discuss the isolation/operational tradeoffs vs shared-schema-with-tenant-id vs DB-per-tenant), the ingestion pipeline (RabbitMQ workers processing millions of bills, partitioned PostgreSQL with monthly partition management and partition pruning), the caching layer (Redis with stampede protection), search (Elasticsearch inverted index), the API/proxy layer (YARP for observability), and the deployment (AKS, distributed sessions). For each, state the tradeoff you actually made. An interviewer who hears you reason about *your own* production system at this level knows immediately you operate at senior+. Rehearse it until it's a tight 10-minute narrative.

design-5-senior-signals-and-the-mistakes.title: Senior signals & the mistakes that sink candidates — overview
design-5-senior-signals-and-the-mistakes.body:
**Signals that read as senior/staff:**
- Driving the conversation and scoping *before* designing.
- Quantifying with estimation and letting numbers pick the hard problem.
- Naming the tradeoff and cost of every choice ("Redis here cuts read latency but adds a failure mode and a consistency question — I'll fail open").
- Choosing the *weakest* consistency the use case tolerates, deliberately.
- Going deep on mechanisms (outbox, consistent hashing, fan-out hybrid) not logos.
- Discussing failure modes and how the system degrades.

**Mistakes that sink it:**
- Jumping to a diagram before requirements.
- Name-dropping tech without justifying it ("I'll use Kafka" — why? what does it cost?).
- Ignoring scale (a single DB serving 50k QPS) or over-engineering trivial scale.
- Hand-waving the hard part the interviewer is probing.
- Claiming "exactly-once delivery" without idempotency, or "strongly consistent" everything (expensive and usually unnecessary).
- Forgetting the data flow has to *fail gracefully*.


**Part 2 complete.** Next on the roadmap: **Part 3 — CS Fundamentals**, where I write out the actual answers to every "probe you'll face" question from the master curriculum (OS/concurrency, networking, DB internals, distributed systems, the .NET runtime) — the part that makes you peer-level, not just interview-ready. Say *continue* and I'll write it in full.

lld-4-how-to-drive-an-lld-interview-4-9.title: How to drive an LLD interview — overview
lld-4-how-to-drive-an-lld-interview-4-9.body:
1. **Clarify requirements & scope.** List the core use cases; confirm what's in/out. (Parking lot: multiple floors? vehicle types? pricing model? payment?)
2. **Identify the core entities (nouns)** and their relationships → your classes.
3. **Define behaviors (verbs)** → methods and interfaces.
4. **Apply patterns where variation lives** — name them and why (Strategy for pricing, State for lifecycle, Factory for creation).
5. **Draw the class diagram** — classes, key fields/methods, relationships (has-a / is-a / uses).
6. **Handle concurrency** if the domain needs it (seat booking, spot allocation) — locks, atomic reservation, optimistic concurrency.
7. **Walk a use case end to end** through your classes to prove it works; discuss extensibility ("to add electric-vehicle spots, I'd…").

The grading: clean responsibilities (SRP), extensibility without editing existing code (OCP), justified patterns, and not over-engineering a simple ask.

- **Vending Machine:** **State** pattern (Idle → MoneyInserted → Dispensing → OutOfStock); inventory + change-making.

**Part 4 complete.** You now have OOP judgment, SOLID as refactor drills, the ~15 patterns that appear (with your YARP-proxy, StorePay-adapter, and IExceptionHandler-chain anchors), and five+ worked designs with code.
